summary: DeVault outlines his philosophy for maintaining free software projects, built around three core principles: treating everyone as a volunteer, valuing patches over bug reports, and empowering contributors to work on what they enjoy. He argues that a maintainer's primary role is not writing code but enabling contributors—connecting them with mentors, helping them find work that matches their strengths, and gradually expanding their responsibilities. He deliberately prioritizes long-term project health and contributor satisfaction over short-term user demands, even at the cost of losing users who won't attempt patches themselves.
key_points: Maintainers should never pressure volunteers into work they don't enjoy, and should absorb organizational stress themselves
Users should be empowered to fix their own bugs rather than having maintainers do it for them, even if it takes ten times longer
The maintainer's primary job is people management—matching contributors to work they enjoy and helping them grow
Contributor retention and engagement matter more than satisfying every user request
Contributors should be encouraged to work on other projects too, building deeper and more diverse experience
money_quotes: One patch is worth a thousand bug reports.
I'd rather have something done slowly, correctly, and by a contributor who is enjoying their work than quickly and by a contributor who is burnt out and stressed.

I would much rather help someone familiarize themselves with the codebase and tooling necessary for them to solve their own problems and send a patch, even if it takes ten times longer than fixing the bug myself.
I would rather lose every user who is unwilling to attempt a patch than invest the resources of my contributors into work they're uninterested in.

I have never found a user who, even if they aren't comfortable with programming or the specific technologies in use, has been unable to solve a problem which they were willing to invest time into and ask questions about.
Getting and supporting contributors is the single most important thing you can do for your project as a maintainer.
Things like commit access are never a function of rank or status, but of enabling people to do the things that they're good at.
key_insight: The most productive thing a FOSS maintainer can do is not write code, but cultivate contributors by connecting them with work they enjoy and helping them grow.
